ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for chapati; A000F (Flatbread) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium plain chapati weighs approximately 40 g and is typically served as an accompaniment to curries, dals, or vegetable dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati, plain — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) lists plain chapati (made from whole wheat atta, water, and salt, cooked on a tawa) as a distinct entry. Values used directly without adaptation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chapati, whole wheat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 for plain whole-wheat chapati is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. GL per serving = (52 × 57.1 × 40/100) / 100 = 11.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~190 mg/100 g); portion size and accompaniments
hypertension.guidance: Plain chapati contributes moderate sodium (~76 mg per 40 g piece); individuals with hypertension should monitor total sodium intake from accompaniments such as curries and pickles, which typically contribute far more sodium than the chapati itself.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) and significant carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 52 and approximately 22.8 g of carbohydrate per 40 g serving, plain chapati has a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one or two pieces and pair with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, whole-grain fibre, and absence of trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain chapati made from whole wheat atta is low in saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), contains no trans fat, and provides dietary fibre (3.9 g/100 g), making it a heart-friendly staple when consumed in appropriate portions as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain chapati (also spelled chapatti, chappati, or chappatti) is a traditional South Asian unleavened flatbread made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a small amount of salt. The dough is kneaded until smooth, divided into small balls, and rolled into thin rounds approximately 15–20 cm in diameter. Each round is cooked on a dry, thick iron griddle (tawa) over medium-high heat, pressed lightly to encourage even cooking, and may be briefly held over an open flame to puff up. The result is a soft, slightly chewy flatbread with a mild, nutty whole-wheat flavour and light char marks on the surface. In Singapore, plain chapati is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as an accompaniment to curries, dals, butter chicken, and vegetable dishes. Nutritionally, chapati is a moderate-GI (GI 52) carbohydrate source providing approximately 297 kcal, 9 g protein, 3.7 g fat, and 57.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g, along with meaningful dietary fibre (3.9 g/100 g), iron (2.7 mg/100 g), and B vitamins. Its whole-grain composition makes it preferable to refined-flour breads for blood glucose management and cardiovascular health. It is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and halal, but contains gluten and is unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chapati?
What is the GI of chapati?
How much carbohydrate in one chapati?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), which provides an exact entry for plain chapati. GI value of 52 is from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) in Diabetes Care. Ontology codes are verified against FoodOn and SNOMED CT. All mandatory multilingual names for Indian cuisine served in Singapore are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not separately reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not separately reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as plain chapati contains no added hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and consistent with whole-wheat flour composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as chapati is a plant-based food containing no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 as whole wheat flour contains negligible vitamin C after cooking; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as chapati is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not separately reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (52 × 22.84) / 100 = 11.9, where carbohydrate per 40 g serving = 57.1 × 0.4 = 22.84 g. The input food_name 'Plain Chappatti' uses a double-t spelling variant; canonical English name normalised to 'Plain Chapati'. Food group updated from 'Mixed' to 'Grains and staples' to reflect the actual food category.
